trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 4 DataParallel training also failed with collate error 'Trying to resize storage that is not resizable'. Analysis: Cause is dataset returning non-contiguous tensors (np.fliplr output non-contiguous, and possibly resizing bug where original h,w not updated leading to inconsistent crop sizes). Decision: Fix dataset.py (update h,w after resize, ensure contiguity with ascontiguousarray, copy after flip). Also adjust train.py loss handling for DataParallel (use loss.mean() only if tensor not scalar). Run 5-epoch training with batch_size=4 to be safe, log validation loss, save best checkpoint, then submit eval to get baseline mIoU.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 5 collation error 'Trying to resize storage that is not resizable' occurred again. Identified root cause: validation dataset does NOT use random crop, so images have varying sizes; batch_size=4 causes collation failure when stacking tensors of different shapes. Training data itself is fine because of crop to 512x512. Analysis: Need to fix validation loader to batch_size=1, and save checkpoints properly for eval. Also ensure custom collate to guarantee contiguity. Decision: Rewrite train.py with val batch_size=1, proper checkpoint saving (state_dict + id2label), and explicit model.eval() handling. Then run 5 epochs and submit eval to get baseline mIoU.

Observation: Iter 6 collate_fn still fails with image shape mismatch (511x512 vs 512x512). Analysis: Random crop sometimes produces 511x512 dimensions despite our resize logic—likely due to edge-case rounding or PIL crop behavior. This prevents stacking in batch_size>1. Decision: Fix dataset.py to enforce exact 512x512 crop (pad if necessary, then random crop of exact size). Keep batch_size=4 and collate_fn. Then train 5 epochs, save best checkpoint, submit eval to get baseline mIoU and measure iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 7 training ran for 2.8h on 4 GPUs (DataParallel) but was killed with exit code 137 (likely OOM). Training curves show loss decreasing but NaN at step 890. No checkpoint saved. Analysis: DataParallel with SegFormer's internal loss computation caused memory issues and process kill. After 7 iterations we still have no baseline mIoU. Decision: Switch to DDP (torchrun) with manual loss computation, proper DistributedSampler, and validation on rank 0 only. This is a proven approach for segmentation with HF models. Use batch_size=4 per GPU (total 16). Train 2 epochs to get baseline mIoU and measure iteration time. Update predict.py to resize images to short side 512, infer, and resize masks back to original size. Submit eval after training.

Observation: After 8 iterations, still no baseline mIoU due to repeated DDP/DataParallel crashes (OOM, SIGABRT) with SegFormer B5. Training curves show loss decreasing but process killed. Analysis: SegFormer B5 is too memory-heavy or unstable under multi-GPU training in this environment. Need a simpler, lighter model to get a working pipeline and baseline score. Decision: Switch to DeepLabV3 with ResNet50 backbone (pre-downloaded, lightweight). Use single-GPU training to eliminate multi-GPU bugs. Train 5 epochs, validate, save best checkpoint, submit eval. Also rewrite predict.py for robust multi-GPU inference.

Observation: Iter 9 training crashed with TypeError: _SimpleSegmentationModel.forward() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given. This is because we call model(images, masks) but torchvision DeepLabV3 expects only images. Analysis: We need to compute loss manually using cross-entropy with ignore_index=255. Additionally, predict.py will fail on variable-sized images with batch_size>1 due to default collate; need a padding collate. Decision: Rewrite model.py, train.py (fix forward + add val mIoU), and predict.py (padding collate, multi-GPU). Train 5 epochs and submit eval to finally get a baseline mIoU.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 12 eval failed because predict.py attempted to download resnet50 weights in the offline eval environment. The torchvision deeplabv3_resnet50 defaults to weights_backbone=IMAGENET1K_V1, triggering a download. Analysis: Must explicitly set weights_backbone=None to prevent any download. Our trained checkpoint from iter 10 (DeepLabV3, val mIoU 0.273) contains the fine-tuned weights, so we can load them into an uninitialized model without any network call. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to pass weights_backbone=None, remove TORCH_HOME override, and submit eval immediately with the best checkpoint. No training command needed; time remaining only 0.2h, must secure a score.
